nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reef environments including lagoons, protected reef slopes, and upper reef zones with moderate to high water flow

decorPreferences: exposed ledge or rock face for mounting

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Has been documented as an aggressive feeder that will accept supplemental target feedings of mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, zooplankton, and other small meaty foods. Amino acids and phytoplankton have been reported to enhance coloration and growth. Weekly or bi-weekly target feeding is generally recommended but not mandatory.

goodWith: corals placed well out of reach (15+ cm clearance)
avoidWith: adjacent LPS corals without adequate spacing
adjacent SPS corals without adequate spacing
soft corals placed within sweeper range

summary: The Hydnophora Coral is a fast-growing stony coral from the Indo-Pacific, available in branching and encrusting forms. It is widely considered one of the more aggressive stony corals in the hobby, capable of stinging and chemically damaging neighboring corals at close range.
careNotes:
Hydnophora is a genus of roughly six species traded in the aquarium hobby almost always under the catch-all name Hydnophora sp. The two forms most commonly encountered are H. exesa, which grows as a flat encrusting sheet, and H. rigida, which produces upright branching columns sometimes called Velvet Horn Coral.

Classification: Hydnophora sits at an unusual boundary in the hobby. Its skeleton is fully calcified like an SPS (small polyp stony) coral — meaning it builds a hard, rigid calcium carbonate structure — but its polyp structure and care behavior are closer to LPS (large polyp stony) corals. It belongs to family Merulinidae and is generally sold alongside LPS corals in the aquarium trade.

Aggression — Hydnophora is widely considered among the more aggressive stony corals in the aquarium trade:
• After lights out, it has been known to extend sweeper tentacles reaching approximately 5-8 cm (2-3 inches) that can sting and damage neighboring corals.
• Even without extended tentacles, Hydnophora has been documented extruding mesenterial filaments — thin threads laden with digestive enzymes — when neighboring corals come within 2-4 cm, causing significant tissue damage or death to the adjacent coral.
• A minimum clearance of 15-20 cm (6-8 inches) from all neighboring corals is generally advised.
• Growth rate is reported as relatively fast; colonies should be monitored and fragged periodically to prevent them from encroaching on neighbors.

Lighting: New specimens are generally advised to be acclimated gradually, starting in moderate light (around 150 PAR) and slowly moved to the target zone of 200-300 PAR. Color tends to fade in insufficient light.

Water chemistry: Like all stony corals, Hydnophora consumes calcium and alkalinity as it grows. Sudden alkalinity swings should be avoided; consistent dosing or a calcium reactor is recommended in tanks housing multiple stony corals. Nitrates between 1-10 ppm and phosphate between 0.01-0.1 ppm are generally acceptable.

Growth: Branching morphs (H. rigida) grow upward and outward; encrusting morphs (H. exesa) spread laterally across rockwork. Either form can become a sizable colony in a well-maintained system.

CITES: Hydnophora sp. is listed under CITES Appendix II, meaning export is regulated. Aquaculture specimens from facilities such as ORA and ECC are readily available and are generally preferred over wild-collected colonies, as they tend to acclimate more readily.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method in captivity. Branches of H. rigida can be cut with bone cutters or a bandsaw and mounted on frag plugs; encrusting H. exesa morphs can be chipped away from the base rock. Aquacultured specimens from producers such as ORA and ECC are available through a number of specialty retailers and tend to acclimate more readily to aquarium conditions than wild-collected colonies.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Encrusting Horn Coral
primary.notes: Primary source for scientific name (Hydnophora exesa), care level (moderate), temperament (aggressive), water parameters (temperature 72-78°F, SG 1.023-1.025, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-12), feeding notes, and aggression description.

additional.name: Bulk Reef Supply – WWC Glowstick Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters: temperature 75-78°F, SG 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-9 dKH, Ca 380-450 ppm, Mg 1300-1450 ppm. Lighting listed as 75-250 PAR; flow as moderate-to-high. Confirmed aggressive temperament and fast growth rate.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn – ORA Green Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked parameters: temp 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, salinity 32-35 ppt (≈SG 1.025-1.027), kH 8-12 dKH, Ca 350-450 ppm, Mg 1250-1350 ppm. PAR ~250 cited. Confirmed high aggression (described as 'potent sting that kills any coral it touches') and feeding behavior.

additional.name: Vivid Aquariums – Green Hydnophora
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temp 75-80°F (24-27°C), SG 1.024-1.026, pH 8.1-8.4, kH 8-9.5, Ca 420-440, Mg 1260-1350. PAR 250-350. Confirmed 2-3 inch (5-8 cm) spacing requirement and aggressive temperament. Noted Indo-Pacific native range.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Hydnophora rigida
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Order Scleractinia, Family Merulinidae), native range (Indo-Pacific: Australia, Philippines, Fiji, Indonesia, Samoa, Solomon Islands, Madagascar), and depth range (1-30 m). Habitat: lagoons and protected reef slopes.
